serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Y3 Crispy Chicken Rice Tom Yam typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a serving of white rice topped with crispy deep-fried chicken pieces and tom yam sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Y3 Crispy Chicken Rice Tom Yam — HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient record
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Y3 Crispy Chicken Rice Tom Yam (cr_id G00010411). All per-100g values are sourced at confidence 0.95 as recorded in the input per_100g_meta fields. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried chicken with rice (USDA FDC ID 171477 and 168880 composite) at approximately 1.2 g/100g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white rice (boiled) and analogous fried chicken rice composite
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Y3 Crispy Chicken Rice Tom Yam. GI of 72 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white rice (GI 72, glucose reference), which constitutes the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. The crispy batter coating adds a modest refined-carbohydrate load but does not materially alter the overall GI relative to the white rice base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 72 × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 52.9 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 = 38.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 324 mg/100g from tom yam paste and fish sauce, with insufficient potassium (182 mg/100g) and fibre (1.0 g/100g) offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request sauce on the side or reduced tom yam paste and fish sauce to keep sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day ceiling; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1134 mg sodium, representing over half the daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice base with GI 72 and moderate carbohydrate load (15.1 g/100g); crispy batter adds refined carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should reduce the rice portion or substitute brown rice to lower the glycaemic load, request grilled rather than crispy fried chicken to eliminate batter-derived refined carbohydrate, and ask for reduced tom yam sauce to minimise added sugar from the paste.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes saturated fat (~1.2 g/100g estimated) and dietary cholesterol (13 mg/100g); high sodium load from tom yam paste and fish sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request grilled chicken to reduce saturated fat from deep-frying, and ask for sauce on the side to control sodium intake, given that a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 1134 mg sodium and an estimated 4.2 g saturated fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that directly exacerbate joint inflammation; chilli as a nightshade ingredient may worsen sensitivity in susceptible individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the deep-fried preparation and request grilled or baked chicken to eliminate AGE formation; fish sauce and tom yam paste also contribute sodium that may worsen RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Y3 Crispy Chicken Rice Tom Yam is a Singaporean hawker-style dish that combines deep-fried crispy chicken pieces served over steamed white rice and dressed with a tangy, spicy tom yam sauce. The dish draws on Thai culinary tradition — specifically the aromatic tom yam flavour profile built from lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, chilli, fish sauce, and lime juice — adapted into a rice-plate format popular in Singapore food courts and casual dining outlets. The chicken is coated in a seasoned batter and deep-fried to a golden, crunchy exterior, yielding a contrast of crisp coating and juicy meat. The tom yam sauce delivers a characteristic hot-sour-savoury punch. Per 100 g, the dish provides 116 kcal, 4.96 g protein, 4.0 g fat, and 15.12 g carbohydrate, with 324 mg sodium — a level that warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The white rice base carries a high glycaemic index (GI ~72), making portion control important for individuals with type 2 diabetes. Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which is a concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is halal-suitable and dairy-free but contains fish-derived ingredients (fish sauce) and potential shellfish traces in commercial tom yam paste. It is typically consumed as a lunch or dinner main course in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for all reported fields. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite for fried chicken and white rice. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database white rice reference (GI 72), which is the dominant carbohydrate driver. All mandatory multilingual names provided.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Y3 branded dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried chicken (FDC ID 171477) and cooked white rice (FDC ID 168880) in approximate dish proportions; this value should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database white rice (boiled) reference; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in published literature as of 2026-04-16. The dish is sold under the Y3 brand at Singapore food courts; the cr_id G00010411 is the HPB canonical record identifier.
